content.text: is the art of selecting and designing demonstrations that effectively teach the model the pattern, format, and reasoning you want it to follow. As research shows,
the specific examples chosen are critical
Good examples are like giving the AI a
— they show the model how you want something done before it does the task itself.
Research from Min et al. (2022) reveals that
the specific examples chosen are critical
for few-shot prompting performance. The key findings:
— Show the model the range of possible outputs (even if labels are randomly assigned)
— Match the distribution of examples to what you expect in real use
— The format you use plays a key role in performance, even with random labels
: Few well-chosen examples are better than many random ones
: Keep the format identical across all examples
: Match example distribution to real-world distribution
1. Show the Pattern, Not Just the Answer
you want, not just the surface answer. Show the reasoning process or structure.

Email: "I was double charged."
→ Category: Billing

Email: "I was double charged this month and my receipt shows two payments."
→ Category: Billing
Reason: The email is about incorrect charging.
2. Use Consistent Format Across All Examples
The format you use plays a key role in performance
— even random labels are better than no labels at all. Keep the format identical across all examples.

Email: "I was double charged."
→ Category: Billing

Email: "The app crashes."
Category: Bug
Reason: The app is crashing.

Email: "I was double charged."
→ Category: Billing
Reason: The email is about incorrect charging.

Email: "The app crashes."
→ Category: Bug
Reason: A specific feature is crashing.
(what the model will see most often)
(looks like X but is actually Y)

Email: "The app crashes when I click Export."
→ Category: Bug
Reason: A specific feature is crashing.

Email: "Export is too slow."
→ Category: Bug (not Feature, because it's a performance issue)

Email: "I'd love an Export feature."
→ Category: Feature (because it's a new capability request)
This teaches the model to distinguish between:
Selecting random labels from a true distribution
(instead of uniform distribution) helps. Match the distribution of examples to what you expect in real use.
If 70% of your emails are "Billing" and 30% are "Bug", your examples should reflect that ratio:

Email: "I was double charged." → Billing
Email: "Payment failed." → Billing
Email: "The app crashes." → Bug

Email: "I was double charged." → Billing
Email: "The app crashes." → Bug
Email: "I want dark mode." → Feature
Email: "I don't understand." → Other
that show edge cases and ideal style. More than 5 may consume too much context.
Too few (<3): May not teach the pattern well
Too many (>5): Wastes tokens, may confuse the model
examples that match what you'll actually see in production. Don't use obviously fake or simplified inputs.

Email: "I was double charged this month and my receipt shows two payments."
→ Category: Billing
Reason: The email is about incorrect charging.
to clearly separate examples from instructions. This is especially effective with Claude, which responds well to

<examples>
Email: "I was double charged."
→ Category: Billing
Reason: The email is about incorrect charging.

Email: "The app crashes."
→ Category: Bug
Reason: A specific feature is crashing.
</examples>
Good Example Patterns by Task Type

Task: Classify emails into Billing, Bug, Feature, or Other.

<examples>
Email: "I was double charged this month and my receipt shows two payments."
→ Category: Billing
Reason: The email is about incorrect charging.

Email: "The app crashes every time I click 'Export to CSV'."
→ Category: Bug
Reason: A specific feature is crashing.

Email: "It would be great if the dashboard could export directly to Google Sheets."
→ Category: Feature
Reason: User is requesting a new capability.
</examples>

Task: Extract name, email, and phone from text.

<examples>
Text: "Hi, I'm John Smith. You can reach me at john@example.com or 555-1234."
JSON: {
  "name": "John Smith",
  "email": "john@example.com",
  "phone": "555-1234"
}

Text: "Contact Sarah Johnson at sarah.j@company.com. Her phone is 555-5678."
JSON: {
  "name": "Sarah Johnson",
  "email": "sarah.j@company.com",
  "phone": "555-5678"
}
</examples>
Shows exact JSON format (with proper indentation)
Covers different input patterns (name first, email first, phone first)

Task: Write a follow-up email after a sales call.

<examples>
Input: "Met with Sarah from TechCorp. She's interested in our analytics feature but worried about pricing."
Output: "Hi Sarah, Thank you for taking the time to speak with me yesterday. I enjoyed learning about TechCorp's goals and discussing how our analytics feature could help you track customer behavior more effectively. I understand pricing is a concern, and I'd be happy to explore options that fit your budget. Would you like to schedule a follow-up call to discuss next steps?"

Input: "Spoke with John from DesignCo. He needs a quick solution for client reporting."
Output: "Hi John, Thank you for the great conversation today! I'm excited about the possibility of helping DesignCo streamline your client reporting process. Our analytics platform can generate reports in under 5 minutes, which sounds like exactly what you need. Let's set up a time this week to get you started. When would work best for you?"
</examples>
Shows structure (acknowledgment → value proposition → call to action)
Shows variety in how meetings are described
Task: Explain what each function does in plain English.
Explanation: This function takes two numbers and returns their sum.
Explanation: This function takes a name and prints a greeting message.

Input: "I love this product!"
Output: Positive

Input: "This is terrible."
Output: Negative

Input: "It's okay."
Output: Neutral
All three categories (Positive, Negative, Neutral) are represented.
Add examples that show where the categories

Email: "The app crashes when I click Export." → Bug

Email: "Export is too slow." → Bug (not Feature, it's a performance issue)

Email: "I'd love an Export feature." → Feature (it's a new capability)
This teaches the model to distinguish:
Bug: feature is broken or slow
Include examples with different input patterns so the model learns the

Text: "Hi, I'm John Smith. You can reach me at john@example.com or 555-1234." → (name first)

Text: "Contact Sarah Johnson at sarah.j@company.com." → (name before email)

Text: "Her phone is 555-5678. Contact Mike at mike@email.com." → (phone before email)
Don't use simplified or obviously fake inputs. Use text that matches what you'll actually see.

The specific examples chosen are critical
The format matters more than label accuracy

Label space and distribution are important
